heroOverview: Peritonitis is acute inflammation of the peritoneum — the smooth membrane lining the abdominal cavity — and carries an in-hospital mortality of 10-40% when caused by a perforated viscus or generalised bacterial contamination, making it one of the most time-critical abdominal emergencies in surgery. The unspecified designation (ICD-10: K65.9) applies when the aetiology has not been identified at the time of coding. Bacterial contamination from a perforated appendix, perforated peptic ulcer, or ruptured diverticulum is the commonest cause worldwide, though secondary peritonitis following abdominal surgery and spontaneous bacterial peritonitis in patients with cirrhosis and ascites are clinically important variants. The cardinal presentation is a rigid, board-like abdomen with severe generalised pain that worsens with movement; rapid diagnosis, source-control surgery, and early broad-spectrum antibiotics are the pillars of management.

keyStats.prevalence: Secondary peritonitis from a perforated viscus occurs in 1-2 per 10,000 adults per year in high-income countries; spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) affects 10-30% of cirrhotic patients with ascites annually
keyStats.demographics: Secondary peritonitis peaks in adults aged 40-70 (in line with peptic ulcer, diverticular disease, and colorectal cancer prevalence); SBP peaks in patients with decompensated cirrhosis
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age at diagnosis approximately 55 years for secondary peritonitis; younger in perforated appendicitis
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 90,000-130,000 emergency laparotomies for peritonitis are performed annually in the United States

definition: Peritonitis (ICD-10: K65.9, unspecified) is an acute inflammatory process of the peritoneal membrane, classified by cause and extent. Secondary peritonitis, by far the most common form, results from perforation or transmural necrosis of a hollow viscus — appendix, colon, stomach, small intestine, gallbladder, or uterus — spilling bacteria-laden contents into the sterile peritoneal cavity. Primary peritonitis (spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, SBP) occurs without a perforated viscus, most often in patients with liver cirrhosis whose ascitic fluid becomes infected by gut bacteria translocating across a compromised bowel wall or via the bloodstream. Tertiary peritonitis is a persistent, low-grade infection seen after initial source control in critically ill patients, usually with resistant organisms. The peritoneum responds to invasion with massive exudation of fluid, fibrin deposition, and an acute-phase response; without treatment, this leads to systemic inflammatory response syndrome, septic shock, multi-organ dysfunction, and death. Gastroenterology manages SBP medically; general and colorectal surgeons handle secondary peritonitis that requires operative source control.

typesClassification.description: Caused by perforation or rupture of an intra-abdominal organ — most commonly the appendix, colon (diverticular disease or cancer), or peptic ulcer — releasing bacteria and digestive contents into the peritoneum. Requires urgent surgical source control.

Infection of ascitic fluid without a surgical source; occurs almost exclusively in patients with liver cirrhosis. Diagnosed by ascitic neutrophil count above 250 cells/mm³ and treated with antibiotics without surgery.

Persistent or recurrent peritonitis after apparently adequate source control, typically in ICU patients infected with resistant organisms such as Candida, Enterococcus, or Pseudomonas; carries the highest mortality.

Caused by irritant chemical contact — bile from a perforated bile duct, gastric acid from a perforated ulcer, or blood — before bacteria colonise the fluid. May evolve rapidly to bacterial peritonitis.

Confined to a peritoneal compartment, forming a walled-off abscess (such as a pericolic or periappendicular abscess); less systemic illness but may progress to generalised peritonitis if the abscess ruptures.

primarySymptoms: Severe, constant abdominal pain that begins focally (e.g., at the site of perforation) and rapidly becomes generalised, typically worsening with movement and partially relieved by lying still with the knees drawn up.
A rigid, board-like abdomen on palpation, caused by involuntary guarding of the abdominal wall musculature in response to parietal peritoneal irritation — one of the most reliable clinical signs.
Rebound tenderness: pain that is worse when the examining hand is quickly released than when pressure is applied, signalling parietal peritoneal inflammation.
Fever above 38.5°C with chills and rigors, reflecting bacteraemia and systemic inflammatory response.
Nausea and vomiting that may precede or accompany the pain onset.
Abdominal distension as gas and fluid accumulate in paralytic ileus — the bowel stops moving in response to peritoneal irritation.
Absent bowel sounds on auscultation, reflecting the paralytic ileus that accompanies generalised peritoneal inflammation.
Tachycardia (heart rate above 100 beats per minute) and hypotension as fluid shifts into the inflamed peritoneum cause intravascular volume depletion.
Marked reluctance to move or cough, and shallow rapid breathing to avoid pain from diaphragmatic movement.
In spontaneous bacterial peritonitis: more subtle onset with abdominal discomfort, fever, and worsening liver function in a patient with known cirrhosis and ascites.
earlyWarningSigns: Localised abdominal pain that intensifies over hours rather than waxing and waning, particularly after a known diagnosis of appendicitis, diverticulitis, or peptic ulcer.
Sudden change in pain character — from sharp and colicky to constant and diffuse — which may indicate perforation.
Onset of fever and tachycardia in a patient with liver cirrhosis and ascites.
Loss of normal bowel sounds on clinical exam when abdominal pain is present.
emergencySigns: Rigid, board-like abdomen with generalised tenderness — a surgical emergency requiring immediate assessment.
Hypotension, tachycardia, confusion, or reduced urine output indicating septic shock — call emergency services immediately.
Sudden, severe, diffuse abdominal pain, especially if it followed a period of localised pain from a known abdominal condition such as appendicitis.
Fever above 39°C with abdominal distension and absent bowel sounds in any patient.
Free air under the diaphragm on an erect chest X-ray, confirming visceral perforation — requires emergency surgery.

causes.description: Appendiceal perforation, occurring in about 20-30% of appendicitis cases, is the single most common cause of secondary peritonitis in adults and children; it spills faecolith-laden bacterial flora into the peritoneum, driving a rapid generalised inflammatory response.

A duodenal or gastric ulcer that erodes full-thickness through the wall releases strongly acidic gastric contents; the initial chemical peritonitis evolves within 6-12 hours to bacterial peritonitis as organisms colonise the contaminated fluid.

Diverticulitis complicated by free perforation (Hinchey III-IV) spills faecal contents from the sigmoid colon into the peritoneal cavity, producing faecal peritonitis with high bacterial load and severe systemic sepsis.

Breakdown of an intestinal anastomosis after colorectal, bariatric, or upper gastrointestinal surgery releases bowel contents into the peritoneum; typically presents 5-7 days postoperatively with fever, pain, and rising inflammatory markers.

Bacterial translocation across a compromised intestinal barrier — and bacteraemia from the portal circulation — seeds ascitic fluid with Gram-negative rods (predominantly E. coli and Klebsiella); the compromised immune status of cirrhosis prevents clearance, leading to infection of the ascites.

When blood supply to an obstructed bowel segment is cut off, transmural infarction leads to bacterial translocation and, ultimately, perforation; ischaemic bowel in a volvulus or incarcerated hernia can cause peritonitis within hours.

An obstructing or fungating colorectal tumour may perforate spontaneously or during colonoscopic preparation, releasing faecal flora; peritonitis from colonic perforation carries particularly high mortality given the dense bacterial contamination.

riskFactors.description: Cirrhosis impairs hepatic immune function, reduces ascitic opsonin activity, and promotes intestinal bacterial translocation; each episode of SBP carries 20-30% in-hospital mortality and mandates long-term antibiotic prophylaxis thereafter.

riskFactors.factor: Inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn's disease or ulcerative colitis)
riskFactors.description: Transmural Crohn's disease can fistulise and perforate; toxic megacolon complicating severe ulcerative colitis may perforate the colon and cause fulminant peritonitis.

H. pylori infection and NSAID use drive peptic ulcers; eradication of H. pylori and avoidance of NSAIDs substantially reduce perforation risk, which is otherwise estimated at about 5% of untreated ulcers over 10 years.

Any surgical anastomosis or intestinal manipulation carries a risk of anastomotic leak; complex procedures such as oesophagectomy or low anterior resection have leak rates of 5-15%, each of which can cause peritonitis.

Long-term corticosteroids, chemotherapy, or biological agents mask the symptoms of peritonitis and impair host defences; the typical clinical signs of a rigid abdomen may be attenuated, delaying diagnosis.

riskFactors.factor: Delayed presentation or treatment of appendicitis or diverticulitis
riskFactors.description: Each hour of delay between perforation and surgery raises mortality; prompt diagnosis and operative source control within 12-24 hours of suspected perforation significantly improves outcome.

Diabetic patients have impaired leukocyte function and may have attenuated pain response from neuropathy, leading to late presentation with an advanced infection and higher risk of multi-organ failure.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 40-70 years with peptic ulcer disease, diverticular disease, or colorectal cancer
Patients with liver cirrhosis and ascites at risk for spontaneous bacterial peritonitis
Children and young adults with perforated appendicitis
Post-surgical patients in the first two weeks after any abdominal operation
Immunosuppressed patients on long-term corticosteroids or chemotherapy
Patients on continuous ambulatory peritoneal dialysis (CAPD), who are at risk of device-related peritonitis
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is primarily clinical and must be rapid. The history of a perforated hollow viscus, post-operative course, or known liver cirrhosis with ascites guides the index of suspicion. On examination, an involuntary rigid abdomen with diffuse tenderness and rebound is the hallmark. Vital signs typically show fever, tachycardia, and in advanced cases hypotension — features of septic shock. Blood tests show a markedly elevated white blood cell count and C-reactive protein, metabolic acidosis, and raised lactate when tissue perfusion is compromised. An erect chest X-ray or abdominal X-ray is taken immediately: free gas under the diaphragm on an erect film is pathognomonic of hollow viscus perforation and mandates emergency surgery. CT of the abdomen with intravenous contrast provides more detail on the source, extent of contamination, and any collections; CT is used when the diagnosis is unclear or when operative planning requires delineation of anatomy. For suspected SBP in cirrhosis, paracentesis is diagnostic: an ascitic neutrophil count above 250 cells per mm³ confirms infection, and ascitic fluid culture should be taken before starting antibiotics, though cultures are negative in up to 40% of confirmed SBP. Blood cultures are positive in approximately 50-70% of generalised bacterial peritonitis cases and guide antibiotic de-escalation. Urinalysis is performed to exclude a urological source when pelvic peritonitis is suspected.

diagnosticTests.test: Erect chest X-ray and abdominal X-ray
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects free sub-diaphragmatic gas (pathognomonic of hollow viscus perforation) and identifies bowel dilatation consistent with ileus or obstruction.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You stand or sit upright for 10-15 minutes before the film so any free air has time to rise; the X-ray takes under a minute and is painless.

diagnosticTests.test: CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the source of perforation, extent of peritoneal contamination, intra-abdominal abscess, and free fluid; guides operative planning or non-operative management.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An intravenous contrast injection is given before a 5-10 minute scan; you lie still on the scanner table. Some patients feel warmth from the contrast agent. Results are usually available within 30-60 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count, CRP, and lactate
diagnosticTests.purpose: Markedly elevated white cell count and CRP confirm systemic infection; elevated lactate signals tissue hypoperfusion and predicts severity and mortality.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw from a peripheral or central vein; results in 1-2 hours. In emergency settings these are processed urgently.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identify the causative organism and antibiotic sensitivities to guide appropriate de-escalation after empirical treatment is started.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two or three sets of blood cultures drawn from separate venepuncture sites, ideally before the first antibiotic dose; results take 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures the ascitic fluid neutrophil count and cultures the fluid to diagnose or exclude spontaneous bacterial peritonitis in patients with cirrhosis and ascites.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A fine needle is inserted into the lower abdomen under local anaesthetic to withdraw ascitic fluid; the procedure takes 5-10 minutes and is moderately uncomfortable. Results for cell count are available within an hour; cultures take 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses organ function — renal, hepatic, and haematological — for risk stratification and to guide supportive care in patients with peritonitis-related sepsis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Same blood draw as full blood count; no additional procedure required.

treatmentOverview: Management of peritonitis is a race against time. The three simultaneous priorities are resuscitation, source control, and antimicrobial therapy. Fluid resuscitation with crystalloids targets a urine output of 0.5 ml/kg/hour, a mean arterial pressure above 65 mmHg, and normalisation of lactate; vasopressors are added if fluid alone is insufficient. Broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics covering Gram-negative enteric bacteria, anaerobes, and streptococci are started within one hour of suspected peritonitis — every hour of delay increases mortality by 5-7% in septic shock. Current guidelines (Surviving Sepsis Campaign, IDSA) recommend combinations such as piperacillin-tazobactam, cefoxitin, or carbapenem with metronidazole depending on suspected source and local resistance patterns. Source control — the physical elimination of the infective focus — is the definitive treatment for secondary peritonitis: this means emergency laparotomy or laparoscopy to repair or resect the perforated segment, washout of the contaminated peritoneal cavity, and temporary or definitive closure. In selected patients with contained leaks or small perforations, CT-guided percutaneous drainage of a localised abscess avoids major surgery. Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis is treated medically with intravenous cefotaxime or a third-generation cephalosporin for five days; albumin infusion (1.5 g/kg at diagnosis and 1 g/kg at day 3) halves the rate of hepatorenal syndrome. Long-term norfloxacin or co-trimoxazole prophylaxis reduces SBP recurrence from about 70% to under 20% per year. Peritoneal dialysis-related peritonitis is managed with intraperitoneal antibiotics according to the causative organism, with catheter removal reserved for fungal or persistent bacterial infections.

medicalTreatments.name: Broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics
medicalTreatments.description: Empirical treatment covering enteric Gram-negatives and anaerobes is started immediately after blood cultures are drawn; typical regimens include piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV every 6-8 hours or a carbapenem (meropenem 1 g IV every 8 hours) for hospital-acquired or resistant organisms. Duration is typically 4-7 days after source control.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 30-day mortality is reduced by approximately 20-30% when antibiotics are given within 1 hour of suspected peritonitis-related septic shock; adequate source control plus appropriate antibiotics achieves clinical cure in over 80% of community-acquired secondary peritonitis.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous fluid resuscitation
medicalTreatments.description: Rapid infusion of balanced crystalloids (Ringer's lactate or PlasmaLyte) to restore circulating volume, correct metabolic acidosis, and protect kidney function; guided by lactate clearance and clinical response.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Early goal-directed resuscitation reduces mortality by approximately 15% in septic peritonitis according to the Surviving Sepsis Campaign evidence base; fluid overload must be avoided as it worsens outcome.

medicalTreatments.name: Vasopressors (noradrenaline / norepinephrine)
medicalTreatments.description: Added when adequate fluid resuscitation does not restore a mean arterial pressure above 65 mmHg; noradrenaline is the first-choice vasopressor in peritonitis-related septic shock and is delivered via central venous access.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves haemodynamic targets in approximately 70-80% of patients with septic shock when combined with fluids; mortality benefit is indirect via prevention of organ failure.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous cefotaxime plus albumin infusion (for SBP)
medicalTreatments.description: Third-generation cephalosporins achieve resolution of SBP in 77-94% of patients; albumin co-infusion (1.5 g/kg on day 1, 1 g/kg on day 3) substantially reduces the risk of hepatorenal syndrome — from about 33% to 10% — and improves 3-month survival.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Albumin plus antibiotic therapy reduces in-hospital mortality of SBP from approximately 30% to 15-20% compared with antibiotic alone in randomised trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Norfloxacin prophylaxis (for secondary SBP prevention)
medicalTreatments.description: Once a patient has had one episode of SBP, daily norfloxacin 400 mg reduces annual recurrence from around 70% to under 20% by decontaminating enteric flora and reducing translocation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces 1-year SBP recurrence probability from approximately 68% to 20% in randomised controlled trials; trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is a widely available alternative.

surgicalOptions.name: Emergency laparotomy with source control
surgicalOptions.description: Open surgery to identify and close the perforated viscus, resect gangrenous bowel or a ruptured appendix, and wash out the peritoneal cavity with warm saline; a temporary stoma is created if safe primary anastomosis is not possible (as in faecal peritonitis). The definitive intervention for most secondary peritonitis.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 30-day survival above 85% for community-acquired secondary peritonitis when surgery is performed within 12 hours of diagnosis; mortality rises to 30-40% with 24+ hour delays or faecal contamination.

surgicalOptions.name: Laparoscopic source control
surgicalOptions.description: Minimally invasive repair or resection — laparoscopic appendicectomy or repair of a perforated peptic ulcer using the Graham patch technique — in haemodynamically stable patients with limited contamination. Offers faster recovery and lower wound complication rates than open surgery in appropriate candidates.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Comparable outcomes to open surgery for localised secondary peritonitis in stable patients; conversion to open surgery required in about 10-20% of cases due to adhesions or haemodynamic instability.

surgicalOptions.name: CT-guided percutaneous drainage
surgicalOptions.description: Radiologically guided drainage of a localised peritoneal abscess (e.g., pericolic abscess from diverticulitis, periappendicular abscess) without open surgery; allows interval elective resection after inflammation settles.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Technical success in approximately 85-90% of accessible unilocular abscesses; avoids immediate surgery in around 70-80% of diverticular abscess cases, though around 20-30% eventually need surgery.

surgicalOptions.name: Damage control laparotomy
surgicalOptions.description: Abbreviated surgery in haemodynamically unstable patients: control of haemorrhage and contamination only, with temporary abdominal closure (laparostomy) and planned relook in 24-48 hours when the patient is stabilised in the ICU. Used in the most critically ill.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Improves survival in haemodynamically unstable peritonitis where definitive repair at initial surgery would risk physiological exhaustion; overall mortality in this high-risk subgroup remains 30-50%.

alternativeTreatments.name: Non-operative management for selected perforated ulcers
alternativeTreatments.description: Patients with a contained perforation on CT, no generalised peritonitis, and clinical stability may be managed with nasogastric suction, IV antibiotics, and close monitoring; this approach avoids surgery in carefully selected cases but has a failure rate of about 30%.

alternativeTreatments.name: Interval appendicectomy after non-operative management
alternativeTreatments.description: A periappendicular abscess from a perforated appendix is sometimes treated with antibiotics and drainage alone in the acute phase, with elective appendicectomy 6-8 weeks later; evidence for necessity of interval appendicectomy is mixed, with recurrent appendicitis occurring in about 15-20% of non-operated patients.

whySeeSpecialist: Any patient with suspected peritonitis must be assessed by a surgeon urgently — this is a life-threatening emergency that is not managed outside of hospital. Gastroenterologists lead management of spontaneous bacterial peritonitis in cirrhotic patients. After recovery from peritonitis, a gastroenterologist or colorectal surgeon should investigate and manage the underlying cause to prevent recurrence, whether that is H. pylori eradication, long-term NSAID avoidance, colorectal cancer surveillance, or cirrhosis management.
doctorSelectionGuide: In the emergency setting, care is delivered by whichever surgical team is on call; optimal outcomes depend on a hospital with 24/7 access to emergency surgery, interventional radiology, and intensive care. For ongoing management of the underlying condition, seek a gastroenterologist experienced in liver disease if SBP was the cause, or a colorectal surgeon if diverticular disease or colorectal cancer was the source.
preventionStrategies: Eradicate Helicobacter pylori in patients with peptic ulcer disease to reduce the lifetime perforation risk.
Avoid NSAIDs without proton pump inhibitor cover, especially in older adults or those with a history of peptic ulcer.
Manage diverticular disease with a high-fibre diet and prompt treatment of acute diverticulitis to reduce the risk of free perforation.
Begin long-term norfloxacin prophylaxis after a first episode of SBP in cirrhotic patients.
Undergo regular colonoscopic surveillance in patients with a personal or family history of colorectal cancer to detect and remove polyps before they obstruct or perforate.
Seek same-day care for sudden severe abdominal pain rather than waiting to see if it resolves, as early treatment prevents generalisation.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain a high-fibre diet and adequate hydration to reduce the risk of diverticular disease and peptic ulcer complications.
Avoid alcohol excess, which accelerates liver cirrhosis and raises SBP risk.
Abstain from NSAID use unless prescribed, as NSAIDs are a leading cause of peptic ulcer perforation.
After recovery from peritonitis, follow through on any recommended cancer screening — the underlying cause may indicate a structural risk that needs surveillance.
Report any abdominal pain that returns, worsens, or is accompanied by fever promptly, as recurrent or localised infection may be developing.

recommended: High-fibre diet to prevent constipation and diverticular disease progression
Adequate fluid intake of 1.5-2 litres daily
Low-sodium diet in patients with liver cirrhosis to control ascites
Adequate protein intake during recovery to support wound healing and immune function
avoid: Alcohol, particularly in patients with cirrhosis or peptic ulcer disease
NSAIDs and aspirin without medical guidance in patients with a history of peptic ulceration
Low-residue or very low-fibre diets in patients with diverticular disease
Eating large, infrequent meals that can increase gastric acid secretion in peptic ulcer disease

exerciseGuidelines: During the acute illness, absolute rest is required. In the early recovery period (first 2-4 weeks postoperatively), light walking is encouraged to reduce pulmonary complications and deep vein thrombosis. Strenuous exercise and heavy lifting should be deferred for 6-8 weeks after laparotomy to allow fascial healing. Patients who have had a laparostomy and abdominal reconstruction need individual guidance from their surgical team before returning to full exercise.
prognosis: Prognosis depends critically on the cause, timing of treatment, and patient comorbidities. Community-acquired secondary peritonitis from a perforated appendix or peptic ulcer, treated within 12 hours, carries a 30-day mortality below 5-10% in otherwise healthy adults. Faecal peritonitis from a perforated colon carries a 30-40% mortality even with modern surgical care. Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis in cirrhosis is associated with 20-30% in-hospital mortality and a 70% one-year mortality if untreated or if associated hepatorenal syndrome develops — reflecting the severity of the underlying liver disease rather than the peritonitis itself. Tertiary peritonitis in ICU patients carries the worst prognosis, with mortality above 50%. Survivors of peritonitis are at risk for incisional hernia, adhesive bowel obstruction, and the recurrence of the underlying disease.
recoveryTimeline: After successful surgery for secondary peritonitis, most patients are in hospital for 5-14 days depending on the extent of contamination and whether an ostomy was formed. Oral diet usually resumes on day 2-4. Intravenous antibiotics are converted to oral after 48-72 hours of clinical improvement and completed for a total of 4-7 days. Return to full normal activity takes 4-8 weeks. Patients with an initial ostomy formed during emergency surgery typically have elective reversal at 3-6 months. SBP resolves with five days of antibiotics, but the underlying cirrhosis requires ongoing hepatology management.
complications: Septic shock and multi-organ dysfunction — the most feared immediate complication, affecting 20-40% of patients with generalised secondary peritonitis and requiring intensive care.
Intra-abdominal abscess formation after apparently adequate source control, presenting as persistent fever and rising CRP at 5-10 days postoperatively; managed by CT-guided drainage.
Ileus and adhesive small bowel obstruction — a common late complication of any peritoneal inflammation or laparotomy, affecting an estimated 10-15% of patients at some point.
Incisional hernia — occurs in 10-20% of patients after emergency laparotomy, with higher rates when abdominal closure was delayed (laparostomy).

Hepatorenal syndrome in SBP — acute kidney injury driven by renal vasoconstriction in response to systemic inflammation in cirrhosis; substantially worsens survival and is reduced by albumin infusion.
Wound infection and anastomotic leak after repair — the contaminated operative field increases surgical site infection rates to 20-40% for emergency laparotomy.

supportResources.name: NHS — Peritonitis
supportResources.description: Plain-English UK patient guide explaining what peritonitis is, its causes, and what to expect during hospital treatment.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Appendicitis
supportResources.description: Covers appendicitis, the most common cause of peritonitis, including complications such as perforation and abscess.

supportResources.name: American Association for the Study of Liver Diseases — Cirrhosis Practice Guidance
supportResources.description: Professional guidelines covering SBP diagnosis, treatment, and prophylaxis for clinicians managing cirrhotic patients.

sources.title: Sartelli M et al. 2020 update of the WSES guidelines for the management of acute colonic diverticulitis in the emergency setting. World J Emerg Surg, 2020
Runyon BA; AASLD. Introduction to the revised American Association for the Study of Liver Diseases Practice Guideline management of adult patients with ascites due to cirrhosis 2012. Hepatology, 2013
Solomkin JS et al. Diagnosis and management of complicated intra-abdominal infection in adults and children: guidelines by the IDSA and SSIS. Clin Infect Dis, 2010
Evans L et al. Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2021. Intensive Care Med, 2021
Søreide K et al. Perforated peptic ulcer. Lancet, 2015
NHS — Peritonitis: overview, causes, and treatment
Sort P et al. Effect of intravenous albumin on renal impairment and mortality in patients with cirrhosis and spontaneous bacterial peritonitis. N Engl J Med, 1999

confusedWithConditions.name: Pelvic inflammatory disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: PID causes lower abdominal and pelvic pain with cervical motion tenderness and vaginal discharge in women; peritonitis produces a board-like rigid abdomen that is generalized and is not limited to the pelvis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Crohn's disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Crohn's causes chronic abdominal pain, diarrhoea, and weight loss but does not produce an acute rigid abdomen unless complicated by perforation — at which point peritonitis has supervened.

confusedWithConditions.name: Peritoneal abscess
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A peritoneal abscess is a localised, walled-off collection that produces focal tenderness and fever without the generalised rigidity of diffuse peritonitis; it is managed with drainage rather than emergency laparotomy in most cases.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ulcerative colitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Severe ulcerative colitis causes colicky pain and bloody diarrhoea without the sudden onset rigid abdomen of peritonitis; toxic megacolon complicating UC can progress to perforation and secondary peritonitis.

relatedConditions.name: Peritoneal abscess
relatedConditions.relevance: A peritoneal abscess frequently develops as a complication of peritonitis after initial source control, representing a localised form of the same disease process.

relatedConditions.name: Colorectal cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Colorectal cancer can perforate spontaneously, particularly when obstructing, causing faecal peritonitis as the presenting emergency.

relatedConditions.name: Ulcerative colitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Toxic megacolon complicating severe ulcerative colitis can perforate the colon, precipitating secondary peritonitis requiring emergency surgery.

relatedConditions.name: Crohn's disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Transmural Crohn's disease may perforate or form fistulae that produce localised or generalised peritonitis.

faqs.question: What is peritonitis?
faqs.answer: Peritonitis is inflammation of the peritoneum, the membrane lining the abdominal cavity and covering the organs. It is most often caused by a perforated abdominal organ releasing bacteria into the abdomen. It is a surgical emergency that requires immediate treatment with antibiotics and usually surgery.

faqs.question: What does peritonitis feel like?
faqs.answer: The typical experience is sudden, severe, constant abdominal pain that spreads across the whole abdomen and worsens with any movement. The belly becomes rigid and board-like. Fever, nausea, vomiting, and a rapid heart rate follow quickly. Even light pressure on the abdomen causes intense pain.

faqs.question: Is peritonitis life-threatening?
faqs.answer: Yes. Peritonitis from a perforated viscus carries a mortality of 10-40% even with modern treatment. Outcome depends heavily on how quickly surgery and antibiotics are started; each hour of delay in septic shock increases the risk of death. Prompt emergency care is essential.

faqs.question: What causes peritonitis?
faqs.answer: The most common cause is perforation of an abdominal organ, most often the appendix, a peptic ulcer, or the colon in diverticular disease. In patients with liver cirrhosis, spontaneous bacterial peritonitis occurs when gut bacteria infect ascitic fluid without a perforated organ.

faqs.question: How is peritonitis treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment requires emergency hospital admission. Intravenous antibiotics covering gut bacteria are started immediately. For secondary peritonitis from a perforated organ, surgery to close the perforation and wash out the abdomen is needed urgently. Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis in cirrhosis is treated with antibiotics plus albumin infusion without surgery.

faqs.question: How quickly does peritonitis develop?
faqs.answer: Symptoms of generalised peritonitis typically develop within 6-24 hours of visceral perforation. Initial chemical irritation from gastric acid or bile can produce signs within minutes; bacterial contamination worsens the picture over hours. Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis may have a more gradual onset over 24-48 hours.

faqs.question: What does the board-like abdomen of peritonitis mean?
faqs.answer: A board-like abdomen means the abdominal muscles have contracted involuntarily and rigidly in response to peritoneal irritation. This involuntary guarding is the body's attempt to protect inflamed internal structures from movement. It is one of the most reliable signs of generalised peritonitis on physical examination.

faqs.question: Can peritonitis be treated without surgery?
faqs.answer: Most secondary peritonitis from a perforated viscus requires surgery to remove the source of contamination. Exceptions include a contained perforation on CT scan in a stable patient, which may be managed with antibiotics and drainage. Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis in cirrhosis is treated with antibiotics alone, without surgery.

faqs.question: What is spontaneous bacterial peritonitis?
faqs.answer: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) is an infection of ascitic fluid in a patient with liver cirrhosis, occurring without a perforated organ. It is diagnosed by analyzing ascitic fluid drawn by needle and finding more than 250 white blood cells per cubic millimetre. It is treated with intravenous antibiotics and albumin infusion for 5 days.

faqs.question: What antibiotics are used for peritonitis?
faqs.answer: Empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics covering intestinal bacteria are started immediately; typical choices include piperacillin-tazobactam, cefoxitin with metronidazole, or a carbapenem for resistant organisms. The antibiotic is adjusted once blood or peritoneal fluid cultures identify the specific organism and its sensitivities.

faqs.question: How long does recovery from peritonitis take?
faqs.answer: Hospital stay after surgery for secondary peritonitis is typically 5-14 days. Oral diet usually resumes in 2-4 days. Return to full activity takes 4-8 weeks after laparotomy. Patients who needed a temporary stoma usually have it reversed 3-6 months later. Full recovery depends on the severity and whether complications occur.

faqs.question: What are the complications of peritonitis?
faqs.answer: Complications include septic shock, intra-abdominal abscess after surgery, adhesive bowel obstruction, incisional hernia, and kidney failure (especially in liver cirrhosis patients). Multi-organ failure is the most serious complication and is responsible for most deaths from peritonitis.

faqs.question: Can peritonitis come back?
faqs.answer: Recurrence depends on the underlying cause. Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis recurs in about 70% of cirrhotic patients within a year without antibiotic prophylaxis. Secondary peritonitis from diverticular disease or colorectal cancer can recur if the underlying cause is not treated. Peptic ulcer peritonitis rarely recurs after H. pylori eradication and acid suppression.

faqs.question: What is the difference between peritonitis and appendicitis?
faqs.answer: Appendicitis is inflammation of the appendix; it causes pain that moves from around the navel to the right lower abdomen. Peritonitis is inflammation of the entire peritoneum; it produces a rigid, diffusely tender abdomen. Peritonitis is often a complication of untreated appendicitis after the appendix has perforated.

faqs.question: Does peritonitis always need an operation?
faqs.answer: No. Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis is treated with antibiotics and albumin infusion without surgery. Some cases of localised secondary peritonitis with a contained abscess are treated with percutaneous drainage and antibiotics. However, generalised secondary peritonitis from a perforated viscus almost always requires emergency surgery.

faqs.question: What does free air under the diaphragm on an X-ray mean?
faqs.answer: Free air under the diaphragm on an erect chest X-ray is a sign that gas has escaped from a perforated hollow organ into the peritoneal cavity. It is pathognomonic of visceral perforation and is an indication for immediate emergency surgery unless there is another known explanation such as recent laparoscopy.

faqs.question: Why is early treatment of peritonitis so important?
faqs.answer: Each hour of delay in starting antibiotics and performing source control surgery significantly worsens prognosis. In peritonitis-related septic shock, each hour without appropriate antibiotics is estimated to increase mortality by 5-7%. The window for optimal outcome is typically within 6-12 hours of perforation, making rapid diagnosis and treatment critical.

faqs.question: Can peritonitis be prevented?
faqs.answer: Some cases can be prevented by treating the underlying cause before perforation: eradicating H. pylori in peptic ulcer disease, managing diverticular disease with fibre and prompt treatment of acute episodes, performing colonoscopy to detect colorectal cancer early, and using long-term antibiotic prophylaxis in cirrhotic patients who have had SBP.

faqs.question: What is a peritoneal abscess and how does it differ from peritonitis?
faqs.answer: A peritoneal abscess is a localised collection of pus walled off by adhesions and omentum, producing focal tenderness and fever without the diffuse rigidity of peritonitis. It often develops as a complication after peritonitis and source control surgery. Management involves CT-guided drainage rather than emergency laparotomy in most cases.

faqs.question: What is the mortality rate from peritonitis?
faqs.answer: Mortality varies widely by type and treatment speed: perforated appendicitis treated promptly has a mortality below 5%; faecal peritonitis from a perforated colon carries 30-40% mortality; spontaneous bacterial peritonitis has 20-30% in-hospital mortality in cirrhotic patients; tertiary peritonitis in the ICU has over 50% mortality.

metaDescription: Peritonitis inflames the abdominal lining, causing a rigid abdomen, fever, and sepsis. Learn causes, emergency signs, surgery, and antibiotics. Doctor-reviewed.
